description: Confounder: The model assumes the maximum weight still occurs at t = 1/√6 and that cosine_sa

_meta.explanation: The rule 'The min_snr_5 weighting function assigns higher weights to small t (near data) than to large t (near noise), and its maximum weight occurs at t = 1/√6 ≈ 0.408.' does NOT hold here. The model assumes the maximum weight still occurs at t = 1/√6 and that cosine_sample_median() is just another way to compute a t near that value. However, cosine_sample_median() returns 1 - cos(π/4) ≈ 0.2929, which is small enough that snr = ((1 - t)/t)^2 ≈ 11.7 > 5, so min(snr, 5.0) clamps it to 5, activating the flat region of the weighting function where weight = 5/(snr+1) is no longer maximized at t = 1/√6.
_meta.curriculum_note: Rule 'The min_snr_5 weighting function assigns higher weights to small t (near data) than to large t (near noise), and its maximum weight occurs at t = 1/√6 ≈ 0.408.' holds in the obvious case but breaks here. Distractor B is the hard negative — the rule naively applied.
